flashcards.q: What is globalisation?
flashcards.a: Process of rapid integration and interconnection between countries through foreign trade and investments by multinational corporations (MNCs). Creates integrated global production and market networks.

flashcards.q: Define Multinational Corporation (MNC).
flashcards.a: Company that owns or controls production in more than one nation. MNCs are primary agents of globalisation, spreading production and investments globally.

flashcards.q: What is foreign direct investment (FDI)?
flashcards.a: Investment by foreign companies in production facilities, technology, and assets in another country. MNCs make FDI when establishing factories and offices.

flashcards.q: What is production integration in globalisation?
flashcards.a: Fragmentation of production where different stages occur in different countries based on comparative advantages. Design in one country, manufacturing in another, assembly in a third.

flashcards.q: What is market integration in globalisation?
flashcards.a: Process where trade barriers are removed, allowing goods to flow freely between countries, creating direct competition across borders and price equalization.

flashcards.q: Define liberalisation.
flashcards.a: Removal of government restrictions on trade and investment. Allows free import/export of goods and unrestricted foreign investment, enabling globalisation.

flashcards.q: What is a trade barrier?
flashcards.a: Government restriction limiting foreign trade, such as tariffs (taxes on imports) or quotas (limits on import quantities). Barriers protect domestic producers from competition.

flashcards.q: Define tariff.
flashcards.a: Tax levied on imported goods, making them more expensive. Used to protect domestic producers from foreign competition.

flashcards.q: Define quota.
flashcards.a: Limit on quantity of goods that can be imported. Restricts market access for foreign goods, protecting domestic producers.

flashcards.q: What is the World Trade Organization (WTO)?
flashcards.a: International organization established to liberalize international trade. Sets rules for trade, ensures compliance, and pressures countries to remove barriers. About 160 member countries.

flashcards.q: When did India begin liberalisation?
flashcards.a: Starting around 1991, India began removing barriers to foreign trade and investment, opening the economy to globalisation.

flashcards.q: What role does technology play in globalisation?
flashcards.a: Technology enables globalisation through transportation improvements (containerization, air freight) and communication advances (internet, telecommunications, satellites).

flashcards.q: What is containerization?
flashcards.a: Placing goods in containers that can be loaded onto ships, trains, trucks without unloading. Reduced port handling costs and shipping time dramatically.

flashcards.q: Why is information technology crucial for globalisation?
flashcards.a: Internet enables instant global communication; email reduces costs to negligible levels; computers coordinate complex global production networks; enables coordinating production across continents.

flashcards.q: Name three ways MNCs control global production.
flashcards.a: Setting up own factories; acquiring local companies; placing orders with small producers globally.

flashcards.q: Give an example of production integration.
flashcards.a: Industrial equipment MNC: designs in USA, manufactures components in China, assembles in Mexico/Eastern Europe, provides customer service in India, sells globally.

flashcards.q: Give an example of market integration.
flashcards.a: Chinese toys enter Indian markets at lower prices. Within a year, 70-80% of toy shops replace Indian toys with Chinese. Prices fall; Indian toy makers face losses.

flashcards.q: Who are the main beneficiaries of globalisation in India?
flashcards.a: Consumers (more choice, lower prices), wealthy sections, educated IT workers earning high wages, large Indian companies becoming multinationals, urban areas.

flashcards.q: Who are the main losers from globalisation?
flashcards.a: Small producers unable to compete with MNCs, workers in declining sectors facing job losses, rural populations, vulnerable groups lacking skills for global jobs.

flashcards.q: What percentage of toy shops in India switched from Indian to Chinese toys?
flashcards.a: Within one year of Chinese toys entering market, 70-80% of toy shops replaced Indian toys with Chinese.

flashcards.q: What happened to Ravi's capacitor manufacturing unit?
flashcards.a: When India removed import restrictions (2001), foreign capacitors flooded market at lower prices. His customers switched to imports. Production fell from full capacity to less than half; workers lost jobs.

flashcards.q: In which year did import restrictions on capacitors get removed in India?
flashcards.a: 2001, following India's WTO agreement on liberalisation.

flashcards.q: What are the main characteristics of India's large companies becoming global?
flashcards.a: Companies like Tata Motors, Infosys, Ranbaxy, Asian Paints emerged as multinationals. They invested abroad, created global supply chains, and competed internationally.

flashcards.q: What sectors benefited most from globalisation in India?
flashcards.a: IT and software services (Infosys, Wipro, TCS), automobiles (Tata Motors), pharmaceuticals (Ranbaxy), business process outsourcing.

flashcards.q: What problem exists in WTO's free trade model regarding agriculture?
flashcards.a: Developed countries heavily subsidize farmers (130+ billion annually), allowing artificially low prices. WTO permits this but forbids developing country agricultural subsidies, creating unfair competition.

flashcards.q: How much do developed countries subsidize agriculture annually?
flashcards.a: Approximately 130+ billion dollars annually, particularly USA and European Union.

flashcards.q: What percentage of US population is engaged in agriculture?
flashcards.a: Only 0.5 percent of US population is engaged in agriculture, yet it receives massive government subsidies.

flashcards.q: How do US agricultural subsidies harm developing country farmers?
flashcards.a: US subsidies enable artificially low prices. Cheap US agricultural products flood developing country markets, undercutting local farmers. Meanwhile, WTO forces developing countries to remove support.

flashcards.q: What is meant by 'fair globalisation'?
flashcards.a: Globalisation that benefits all sections of society, not just wealthy and educated. Requires government intervention protecting workers, supporting small producers, and ensuring equitable development.

flashcards.q: What measures are needed for fair globalisation?
flashcards.a: Labour protection, small producer support, fair WTO negotiations, infrastructure expansion, education and skills, social protection, technology transfer from MNCs.

flashcards.q: What is the impact of globalisation on employment?
flashcards.a: Creates jobs in some sectors (IT, exports) but destroys jobs in others (domestic industries facing import competition). Quality of jobs varies—IT workers earn high wages; garment workers earn low wages.

flashcards.q: What are working conditions like for garment workers in Indian export industries?
flashcards.a: Low wages (Rs 150-200 daily), long hours, poor conditions, job insecurity due to temporary contracts, no benefits or legal protections.

flashcards.q: Why do garment workers accept poor employment conditions?
flashcards.a: Lack alternatives. Unemployment or informal sector work offer worse conditions. Workers forced to accept poor terms or face greater hardship.

flashcards.q: What is the relationship between globalisation and inequality in India?
flashcards.a: Globalisation reinforces inequality. Benefits concentrate among educated, wealthy, urban populations. Harms fall on poor, small producers, rural populations, vulnerable groups.

flashcards.q: How has globalisation affected rural areas in India?
flashcards.a: Rural areas remained largely marginalized. Without agriculture export opportunities or IT services, rural areas didn't benefit from globalisation-driven growth.

flashcards.q: What percentage of Indian workforce still depends on agriculture despite globalisation?
flashcards.a: Over 50 percent of India's workforce is employed in agriculture, producing agriculture-based livelihoods.

flashcards.q: How has globalisation affected small farmers in India?
flashcards.a: Small farmers face competition from cheap imported agricultural products. Government support reduced under WTO pressure. Many lost land to debt and foreclosure.

flashcards.q: What is an example of production fragmentation in globalisation?
flashcards.a: A shirt might use Korean fiber, be stitched in Bangladesh, designed in Italy, marketed from London, sold globally. No single country produces the complete product.

flashcards.q: How do tariffs protect domestic producers?
flashcards.a: By taxing imported goods, tariffs increase their price, making local products relatively cheaper and more competitive in domestic markets.

flashcards.q: How do quotas protect domestic producers?
flashcards.a: By limiting import quantities, quotas reduce foreign competition's market access, allowing domestic producers to maintain market share despite higher prices.

flashcards.q: Why is trade barrier removal controversial?
flashcards.a: Barrier removal benefits consumers through lower prices but harms domestic producers through increased competition. Creates winners (consumers, foreign competitors) and losers (domestic producers).

flashcards.q: What is meant by comparative advantage in globalisation?
flashcards.a: Concept that countries specialize in production where they have relative cost advantages, enabling efficient global production networks.

flashcards.q: Give examples of comparative advantages for different countries.
flashcards.a: USA: technology, capital. China: cheap labour. India: skilled IT workers, English-speaking labour. Mexico: geographic access to North American markets.

flashcards.q: How does outsourcing create globalisation?
flashcards.a: MNCs in developed countries place orders with producers in developing countries, creating global value chains and interdependence.

flashcards.q: What is the relationship between MNCs and small local producers?
flashcards.a: MNCs place orders with small producers, controlling prices and conditions. Small producers must accept MNC terms or lose business, creating power imbalance.

flashcards.q: How do MNCs determine prices for small producers?
flashcards.a: MNCs hold market power. They set prices based on competition and cost minimization. Small producers, needing the business, accept MNC-determined prices.

flashcards.q: What happened to Indian toy makers after Chinese competition?
flashcards.a: Within one year, 70-80% of Indian toy shops replaced Indian toys with Chinese. Many Indian toy makers faced losses and closures.

flashcards.q: Why couldn't Ravi's capacitor unit compete with imported capacitors?
flashcards.a: Imported capacitors were cheaper due to economies of scale and lower labour costs in manufacturing countries. Ravi couldn't match prices without destroying profitability.

flashcards.q: What does globalisation mean for countries' food security?
flashcards.a: Countries become dependent on food imports rather than self-sufficient production, creating vulnerability to global price fluctuations and supply disruptions.

flashcards.q: How does globalisation affect environmental sustainability?
flashcards.a: Pollution-intensive industries relocate to developing countries with weak environmental regulations. Global supply chains consume resources; trade increases transportation emissions.

flashcards.q: What is the role of government in managing globalisation impacts?
flashcards.a: Governments must intervene: protect workers, support vulnerable producers, negotiate fairly internationally, invest in infrastructure and education, provide social protection.

flashcards.q: Can developing countries refuse WTO rules?
flashcards.a: Technically yes, but costs are high—trade sanctions, economic isolation, difficulty attracting investment. Most comply despite unfair rules.

flashcards.q: How does globalisation affect labour standards?
flashcards.a: Without WTO enforcement of labour standards, developing countries attract MNCs through low wages and weak regulations. Workers face exploitation enabling profit maximization.

flashcards.q: What is the relationship between globalisation and technology transfer?
flashcards.a: MNCs rarely transfer technology to local companies. High-end R&D remains in developed countries; developing countries remain in low-skill assembly and manufacturing.

flashcards.q: How does globalisation create dependency for developing countries?
flashcards.a: Developing countries depend on MNC investment; must maintain low wages and weak regulations to attract investment; lose policy autonomy.

flashcards.q: What is an alternative to rapid liberalisation?
flashcards.a: Gradual, selective liberalisation protecting vulnerable sectors while opening competitive ones; supporting transition of displaced workers; building local capacity before global exposure.
